---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.left[.size65[**Today's Agenda**]]

.size45[
Building your Argument

Your chosen environmental problem is public (not private) and should be solved collectively (not individually)
]

<br>

.size35[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Record Canvas submissions

2. Bring the Munger book to class

3. Publish Canvas discussion for Tuesday

<br>

.size15[Munger, M. C. (2000). Deciding How to Decide: "Experts," "The People," and "The Market." In *Analyzing Policy: Choices, Conflicts and Practices* (pp. 30–53). W.W. Norton & Company.]




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Assignment 4 (Due Apr 28th)**

Getting Involved in our Community]]

.size40[
1. Find (or create) an opportunity to get actively involved in your issue locally, and

2. Write a report describing what you did, who you worked with and what you learned from the experience.
]

???

As I introduced last week you have a piece of your project you need to get to work on right now!

- Remember, you have to get my sign-off before acting, and

- Your proposal should frame the activity in terms of how it directly ties to your project AND how doing it will help you better complete that project (e.g. deepens your understanding of the nature of the problem, the stakeholders involved, etc.)?

<br>

Quick Brainstorm: Everybody name one thing they could do this weekend to get involved in your chosen problem in our community!

- Something that either connects you with the stakeholders OR deepens your understanding of the problem itself.

- MUST be different than your idea last class!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size40[
**1) The Basics of Problem-Solving in a Community**
- "Politics"
- "Environment"
- **"Policy"**
]

???

This week we have continued our work in the first section of the class focused on laying the foundations for problem-solving.

On Tuesday we moved from politics and the environment to a deep dive into how policy can play a role in solving environmental problems.

Let's refresh our memories on how Munger aims to help us be better problem-solvers using policy.

- SLIDE: Hun-Gats and the "need" for policy



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-cavemen.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

### Use the Hung-Gat thought experiment to explain to me, why do societies need public policies? Why do we need to agree common rules of behavior at all?

- Imagine a primitive tribe that has exhausted all the food it can find that it knows is safe to eat. What do they do next? 

- Illustration of how leaving us each to face community problems on our own leads to sub-optimal outcomes
    - Every person goes out to find food and experiment on themselves to see what is poisonous and what isn't

- Basic survival is maximized when we collaborate, but:
    - No escaping the battles over expertise, and
    - Policy can only ever be as effective as the authority that compels the behavior, and
    - Any society with resource issues will deal with free-riding problems

<br>

SLIDE: So, we need policy, but what design standard should we use?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '68%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png")
```

???

### What are the key lessons of this figure for designing policies to solve an environmental problem?

1. Designing a policy means choosing a "source of wisdom" / "accountability"
    - You can only maximize for one thing at a time!
    - A coherent policy needs to root itself with a clear organizing principle

2. The triangle helps you remember that all policy designs create winners and losers 
    - This can help you anticipate the challenges/problems before they arrive
    - Make your plan better proactively!

3. This triangle offers us a helpful way to understand disputes about your problem in the community
    - As you analyze the stakeholders involved in any environmental problem you will have a better understanding of who they are and what they want if you can figure out where they fit here.

<br>

### Make sense?

SLIDE: A good policy design requires an important argument BEFORE you get to your solution!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '57%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png")
```


???

Ok, so you've identified a problem and may even have a preferred "wisdom" in mind.

### According to Munger, what are the three arguments you have to win BEFORE you get to work on solving your environmental problem?
1. This is a public, not an individual problem,
2. It should be addressed collectively not privately, and
3. We should choose ourselves (democratic processes) or delegate policy-making to experts

VERY importantly, Munger makes a compelling argument that until you convince the involved community of these three things no policy solution is possible!

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '57%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png")
```

???

Let's talk about the roadblocks you may experience as policy designers / problem solvers.

### What kinds of obstacles may push you off the policy track in step 1? In other words, why might an environmental problem be thought of as an individual rather than a public problem?
- p46: No one cares, issue unimportant ordue to civic/religious culture it simply doesn't occur to anyone that government might have a role in this

### What kinds of obstacles may push you off the policy track in step 2 (e.g. a private decision, not collective)?
- Either there is no decision, the group decides this is outside the scope of government OR the group collectively decides not to act

### Questions on this?

<br>

SLIDE: Last important figure from Munger




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '72%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png")
```

???

### Finally, what are the key lessons of this figure for designing policies to solve an environmental problem?

1. When a stakeholder uses language like in these four boxes they are telling you a ton about who they are, what they identify with and what they value!
    - This information is invaluable for designing an effective policy solution
    
2. It will almost certainly be easier to tackle moving someone on one dimension at a time rather than tackling both simultaneously

### Make sense?

<br>

I also really like how in this figure Munger helps to clarify the two dimensions he laid out in the last figure.

- Here we see his definitions of the private vs public and individual vs collective dimensions.

### Is this the only way we could define these?
- Certainly not, but a useful starting place!


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.size60[**Assignment for Today**]

.size40[
**Before class** submit to our Canvas discussion board: 

1. Define your environmental problem

2. Argument: This is a public (not private) problem

3. Argument: We need a collective (not individual) decision
]

???

Today we take all the useful theoretical work from Munger and apply it to your projects with your pre-class work as our jumping off point.

### How did this go? Everybody ready to push forward?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size50[Describe your environmental problem]

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("?", "?", "?"),
  col2 = c("?", "?", "?")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("c", "c"), col.names = c("Public Problem", "Collective Decision")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 40) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "25em") #|>
  # kableExtra::row_spec(c(0, 7), bold = TRUE, background = "#b3ccff")
```

???

Everybody take out a piece of paper.

- Across the top write a simple description of your environmental problem

- Below that make two lists and fill these in with the work you did before class (e.g. list of reasons this is a public problem and a list of reasons this should be a collective decision)

<br>

*IFF warm-up discussion took too long*: *Split into pairs*

Share your lists with each other
- Are the reasons clear?
- Are they compelling?
- Any new ones to add?

*IFF plenty of time*: Go see them all!

Go see and hear everybody elses' work.
- Your job is to make sure every proposal you hear is clear and compelling
- Everyone elses' job is to make sure yours is the same!
- Don't stress counter-arguments yet, just focus on clarity

Go!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size50[Describe your environmental problem]

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("?", "?", "?"),
  col2 = c("?", "?", "?")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("c", "c"), col.names = c("Public Problem", "Collective Decision")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 40) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "25em") #|>
  # kableExtra::row_spec(c(0, 7), bold = TRUE, background = "#b3ccff")
```

???

Ok, everybody take a minute to reflect on what they heard, read and received as feedback
- Use all of that to make any revisions to your proposal you'd like

<br>

### So, how are we feeling? Does everybody have a clear problem definition and argument that this is a public problem that requires a collective decision?

<br>

### Can I have two volunteers willing to put their work up on the board?
- Make sure to give each other lots of space!

<br>

Ok, volunteer 2 take a seat for a sec and we'll start with volunteer 1.

Volunteer 1 present us your current case.

<br>

(SLIDE: Add new columns)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size50[Describe your environmental problem]

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("?", "?", "?"),
  col2 = c("?", "?", "?"),
  col3 = c("?", "?", "?"),
  col4 = c("?", "?", "?")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("c", "c"), col.names = c("Private Problem", "Public Problem", "Collective Decision", "Individual Decision")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 32) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1:4, width = "20em") 
  # kableExtra::row_spec(c(0, 7), bold = TRUE, background = "#b3ccff")
```

???

Volunteer 1, you are now our guinea pig!
- Please add two more columns: Private problem and Individual decision

<br>

Ok class, let's brainstorm. 

### We need reasons this is actually a private problem and/or an individual decision!
- (private problem = my choice has no consequence for your welfare)
- (individual decision = I should be able to choose alone and without interference)

Volunteer 1 will take notes on the board for us!

<br>

Let's do it again!

Volunteer 2, introduce your case and then we'll help you brainstorm!

<br>

### How are we doing? Does everybody understand the exercise?

Take a minute on your own to start these lists for your current case.

Alright, go hear everybody's new lists and help them add more counter-arguments!
- Go!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.pull-left[
```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png")
```
]

.pull-right[

<br>

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png")
```
]

???

Alright, big over-arching questions for us:

### First, what do we do with this information? How do these counter-argument lists about the need for policy help you going forward?

1. This is all about getting good at establishing the baseline you need to overcome to get the stakeholders willing to discuss your issue at all
    - Without this you are shouting into the wind.
    
2. This is also a list of problems to overcome that will anticipate what you're going to hear as you move forward in designing a solution.
    - It will be VERY helpful to you to keep arguments about the NEED for policy SEPARATE from the arguments about the design of the policy.
    
### Make sense?

<br>

### Does anybody have a list of counter-arguments that are particularly daunting or difficult to address? Let's discuss!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Assignment 4 (Due Apr 28th)**

Getting Involved in our Community]]

.size40[
1. Find (or create) an opportunity to get actively involved in your issue locally, and

2. Write a report describing what you did, who you worked with and what you learned from the experience.
]

???

### How is planning for the community engagement project going? Need ideas?

Don't stall on this!

- Your semester is only going to get busier AND the information you gather from doing this will be MUCH more useful early in your project's development!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.size70[**Assignment for Tuesday**]

.size45[
**Before class** submit to Canvas:

What are **three** key elements you argue are necessary to **successfully navigate** a domestic policy-making process?
]

???

Next week we explore processes of policy-making and problem-solving.

### Questions on the assignment?
